title: Generalizing Verifiable Instruction Following
authors: Valentina Pyatkin, Saumya Malik, Victoria Graf, Hamish Ivison, Shengyi Huang, Pradeep Dasigi, Nathan Lambert, Hannaneh Hajishirzi (Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence; University of Washington)
venue: NeurIPS 2025, Datasets & Benchmarks Track (per arXiv page comments); arXiv preprint v3 (2025-11-11), arXiv:2507.02833
claim: Most state-of-the-art language models strongly overfit to the small, fixed set of 25 verifiable constraints in IFEval and fail to generalize to unseen output constraints; the paper introduces IFBench, a 300-prompt benchmark of 58 new out-of-domain verifiable constraints, on which leading models such as GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7/4 Sonnet, and Qwen3-32B score below 50%. The paper further shows that RLVR (GRPO) training with diverse, multi-constraint training data ('IF-RLVR') substantially closes this gap in-domain and improves (but does not saturate) out-of-domain IFBench generalization.
novelty: The authors present three contributions relative to prior work: (1) IFBench itself — 58 new, human-curated, programmatically verifiable constraints (7 categories: count, ratio, words, sentence, format, custom, copy) paired with unseen WildChat prompts, explicitly designed as a held-out generalization test distinct from IFEval's 25 templates, evaluated in both single-turn and a novel multi-turn (3-turn, constraint isolated from task) setting; (2) IFTrain, 29 new hand-annotated training constraints (more than doubling prior train constraint types) with verification functions, disjoint from IFBench's test constraints, to enable studying generalization; (3) IF-RLVR, new RLVR training recipes (GRPO, multi-constraint composition per prompt, wider constraint-variable ranges, reasoning chat templates for base-model training, and a preference-reward-model addition to curb reward hacking/over-optimization). They contrast this with prior IF benchmarks (IFEval, FollowBench, InFoBench) and the only other automatically-verifiable-data work they identify (VFF), positioning IFBench as the first to combine automatic verifiability with an explicit train/test constraint split for generalization testing, plus the first RLVR generalization analysis for precise IF (Sec. 6 Related Work).

protocol.description: IFBench evaluates constraint-following in two distinct interaction structures, described in Sec 2 under 'IFBench': a single-turn setting and a multi-turn setting. Both are single forward-pass-per-turn generation tasks scored by an automatic Python verifier function per constraint (no interactive/iterative feedback loop with the model — 'multi-turn' here means the constraint is isolated across a fixed 3-turn dialogue history the model must respond to once at the end, not a scored back-and-forth correction loop).
protocol.observation_content: Single-turn (Sec 2, 'IFBench' bullet list): 'The "user" prompt consist[s] of a general prompt with task t, concatenated with one or more output constraints c, and the model has to complete t while adhering to c.' The model sees one user turn containing the task plus 1-2 concatenated constraint instructions (from Table 8, e.g. 'Use at least {N} different coordinating conjunctions in the response.') and must produce a single completion; no demonstrations/few-shot exemplars of the constraint are described as being shown in the prompt for the main benchmark evaluation. Multi-turn (same section): 'c is isolated from t in three turns. The first "user" prompt consist[s] of a general prompt with task t and the second turn is an "assistant"'s response to t, r₁. The third turn ("user") asks to rewrite r₁ to comply with a constraint c. The model has to respond to the third turn, given all previous turns as context.' So the model observes: [user: task t] -> [assistant: r₁ (a prior response)] -> [user: 'rewrite r₁ to satisfy constraint c'], and must generate one final assistant turn; there is no scored intermediate feedback — the verifier only checks the final output against constraint c. The LLM-as-judge protocol (Appendix C) used for a separate quality analysis (not part of the core verifiable-accuracy metric) shows a full prompt template: 'Evaluate the response provided below to determine if it meets the specified constraints... Provide an integer score from 1 to 10... Prompt to Evaluate Against: {prompt} Response to Evaluate: {response}... "Score": "<An integer score from 1 to 10.>"'. For IF-RLVR training itself (Sec 3), the model is trained via GRPO with 16 sampled completions per prompt scored by the verifiable reward function (Eq. 1) — this is a training-time sampling loop, not an evaluation-time interactive protocol.

representations.note: The paper does not compare alternative input/output serialization formats for the constraint task itself (no JSON/CSV/table-format ablation analogous to c11's canonicalization framing). The closest 'representation' axes it studies are: (a) constraint-variable range at test vs. train (Sec 4.3: 'different range' vs 'wider range' vs 'same range' — different range consistently scores lower on IFEval; wider range performs comparably or better than same range, Fig. 5); (b) number and diversity of constraints appended per training instance, n in {1,...,6} (Sec 4.1, Table 1: IFBench peaks at 59.5 with n=3 constraints per instance among the 1-6 sweep, though the paper's narrative emphasizes that combining more constraints per instance generally beats training on only up to 3); (c) base-model reasoning chat template vs standard instruct template for RLVR-from-base (Sec 4.7, Appendix F) — reasoning chat template from base models yields better IFBench generalization (Table 6); (d) strict vs loose accuracy scoring, where loose accuracy 'additionally cleans the model's output by removing first/last lines and certain font modifiers' (Sec 2) — this is the paper's own formatting-tolerance dial analogous to c11's need to freeze a trivial normalization.
representations.effect_summary: Range generalization: training on a variable range disjoint from test range hurts IFEval; training on a wider range (superset of test range) matches or beats training on the identical range (Sec 4.3, Fig. 5) — i.e., diversity beyond the test distribution aids generalization, narrow/disjoint ranges hurt. Category removal (Sec 4.4): removing length/keywords categories from training hurts IFEval most; removing change-cases/detectable-format categories barely affects performance (best case 89.65 IFEval accuracy, Fig. 6).

models.name: GPT-4.1 (frontier baseline, no additional training)
key_results: Scores below 50% on IFBench (Sec 1, Introduction: 'leading models such as GPT-4.1 or Claude 3.7 Sonnet score below 50%'); exact numeric value shown only in Figure 1, an unrendered image — precise number unverified — missing from extraction.

models.name: Claude 3.7 Sonnet / Claude 4 Sonnet (frontier baselines)
key_results: Score below 50% on IFBench (Sec 1: 'leading LMs such as Qwen3-32B or Claude 4 Sonnet score below 50%'); also analyzed for correlation between IF accuracy and LLM-as-judge quality scores (Sec 5, 'Trade-offs' subsection) but no exact numeric correlation value or IFBench score given in extracted text beyond the qualitative statement — exact figures unverified — missing from extraction (Figure 1, Figure 8 are images).

models.name: Qwen3-32B (frontier baseline)
key_results: Scores below 50% on IFBench (Sec 1, contributions list, item 1).

models.name: Llama-3.1-Tulu-3-8B family (Tulu-sft, Tulu-DPO, Tulu, IF-RLVR variants)
strategies: SFT baseline; DPO baseline; GRPO/IF-RLVR (multi-constraint RLVR, verifiable rewards)
key_results: Table 3: Tulu-sft IFEval 72.8 / IFBench 20.7; Tulu-DPO IFEval 81.1 / IFBench 25.5; Tulu (final DPO-trained baseline referenced in Sec 1) IFEval 82.4 / IFBench 28.9; IF-RLVR Tulu-DPO-8B IFEval 92.2 / IFBench 44.6.

Abstract/Sec 1 contribution 3: 'we improve the IFeval scores of a Tulu-3-8B model from 82.4 to 92.2, and the IFBench scores from 28.9 to 45.9' (both Table 3's IF-RLVR Tulu-DPO 8b row AND Table 6's 'instruct: tulu3-dpo' after-IF-RLVR IFBench column agree with each other at 44.6, not 45.9 — the abstract's 45.9 does not match either table for the Tulu run; note 45.9 is instead the value in Table 6's separate 'instruct: qwen2.5' after-IF-RLVR IFBench column, so this looks like an unremarked ~1.3-point discrepancy between the abstract's stated Tulu IFBench figure and both tables' matching 44.6, not a second corroborating run).

Table 5 (DPO vs GRPO ablation, strict accuracy): DPO after SFT IFEval 76.89/IFBench 25.2; DPO after DPO IFEval 79.67/IFBench 29.3; GRPO after SFT IFEval 85.77/IFBench 28.6; GRPO after DPO IFEval 89.65/IFBench 30.6 — GRPO consistently beats DPO under matched data/prompts.
Table 6: instruct tulu3-dpo before IF-RLVR IFEval 81.1/IFBench 25.2; after IF-RLVR IFEval 92.2/IFBench 44.6.

models.name: Qwen2.5-7B / Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
strategies: base-model RLVR training with reasoning chat template; instruct-model IF-RLVR; single-turn vs multi-turn RLVR mixes
key_results: Sec 1 contribution 3: 'IF-RLVR increases a Qwen-2.5-7B base model to scores of 87.8 (IFEval) and 54.7 (IFBench)' — note Table 3/Table 6 list 53.7 for IFBench (qwen2.5), a 1-point discrepancy vs the abstract's 54.7, both present verbatim in extracted text.
Table 6: from-base qwen2.5 after IF-RLVR IFEval 87.8/IFBench 53.7; instruct qwen2.5 before IF-RLVR IFEval 74.7/IFBench 31.3, after IF-RLVR IFEval 89.1/IFBench 45.9.

Table 7 (multi-turn RLVR ablation): Qwen2.5-7B trained on single-turn scores single-turn 79.9/55.8 (IFE/IFB) but multi-turn only 57.4/50.0; trained on multi-turn scores multi-turn 65.9/50.0 but single-turn drops to 70.8/41.2; trained on a mix scores single-turn 85.2/51.0 and multi-turn 62.5/51.0. Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct trained on a mix achieves single-turn 89.5... actually per table: single-turn(86.6/54.8), multi-turn(89.5/72.9) — mix training improves both regimes simultaneously for the instruct model.

models.name: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct
strategies: zero-shot instruction following; analyzed for constraint-vs-task prioritization behavior
key_results: Sec 1: 'tends to prioritize the constraint over the instruction' (qualitative). Sec 5: 'Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct's IF accuracy is the most negatively correlated with LLM-as-judge scores, out of this set of models. This indicates that Qwen2.5 tends to focus on the constraints rather than the general instruction.'

models.name: OLMo2 / OLMo2-instruct
strategies: base-model RLVR with reasoning chat template; instruct IF-RLVR
key_results: Table 6: from-base olmo2 after IF-RLVR IFEval 70.4/IFBench 46.6; instruct olmo2 before IF-RLVR IFEval 61.7/IFBench 16.7, after IF-RLVR IFEval 74.5/IFBench 44.6.

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B (base)
strategies: base-model RLVR with reasoning chat template
key_results: Table 6: from-base llama3.1 after IF-RLVR IFEval 88.2/IFBench 54.1 (before-training values 'na' since base models lack a chat template).

models.name: o3 (frontier proprietary reasoning model)
strategies: zero-shot, generation with temperature=1 (their required setting, Appendix G)
key_results: Sec 3.1: 'Our IF-RLVR trained models outperform most of the current state-of-the-art models (besides o3)' — i.e., o3 remains the top performer among out-of-the-box models on this benchmark; exact numeric score not present in extracted text (Figure 1 image) — unverified — missing from extraction.

models.name: DeepSeek R1
strategies: zero-shot with recommended generation settings (temperature=0.6, top_p=0.95, per Appendix G)
key_results: Mentioned only as an evaluation-setting note in Appendix G; no accuracy numbers given in extracted text.

models.name: Preference-pair generation models: Tulu-3-70B, Qwen-72B, Llama-3.1-405B, Llama3-8B, Yi-34B-Chat
strategies: zero-shot completion sampling for constructing DPO preference pairs (up to 5 constraints per prompt)
key_results: Table 4: all-correct rates — Tulu-3-70B 15%, Qwen-72B 26%, Llama-3.1-405B 21%, Llama3-8B 6%, Yi-34B-Chat 10%. Only-one-wrong rates — Tulu-3-70B 35%, Qwen-72B 100%, Llama-3.1-405B 44%, Llama3-8B 16%, Yi-34B-Chat 26%. Sec 4.6: 'Out of all the instances that models get completely correct, 54% have only 1 constraint and only 2% have 5 constraints... Many of the rejected completions do not contain a single passing constraint out of 5 constraints (46%).'

headline_numbers: Leading out-of-the-box models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7/4 Sonnet, Qwen3-32B) score below 50% on IFBench despite scoring 80+% on IFEval at as small as 2B parameters (Sec 1, Introduction).
IF-RLVR improves Tulu-3-8B from IFEval 82.4 -> 92.2 and IFBench 28.9 -> 45.9 (Abstract/Sec 1, contribution 3; cf. Table 3's slightly different 44.6 figure for the same comparison).
IF-RLVR improves a Qwen2.5-7B base model to IFEval 87.8 / IFBench 54.7 (Abstract/Sec 1; Table 3/6 list 53.7, a minor cross-table discrepancy).

Table 1 (n constraints per training instance, qwen2.5 policy): IFBench peaks at 59.5 with n=3, IFEval peaks at 85.8 with n=6; both metrics are non-monotonic across n=1..1000 instances-per-constraint sweep.

Table 5 (DPO vs GRPO, matched prompts/verifier): GRPO after DPO reaches IFEval strict 89.65 / IFBench strict 30.6, versus DPO after DPO's IFEval 79.67 / IFBench 29.3 — GRPO strictly dominates DPO in this controlled comparison.

Table 6: IF-RLVR training from base models with a reasoning chat template achieves IFBench 46.6-54.1 across llama3.1/qwen2.5/olmo2, versus IF-RLVR from already-post-trained instruct models achieving IFBench 44.6-45.9 — reasoning-from-base generalizes slightly better out-of-domain despite comparable IFEval scores (Sec 4.7: 'IF-RLVR with reasoning leads to improved IF generalization').

Sec 4.4: removing change-cases/detectable-format categories from training barely hurts, with the model still achieving IFEval accuracy of 89.65; removing length/keywords categories hurts IFEval most (exact delta not given in text beyond this, remainder in Figure 6 image).

Sec 5, 'Trade-offs': base-policy completions score higher on LLM-as-judge quality (avg 7/10) than IF-RLVR-trained completions (avg 6.4/10) when constraints are stripped from the judged prompt, while verifiable accuracy is higher for the RLVR model — a documented quality/constraint-adherence trade-off.
failure_modes: Overfitting to a fixed constraint template set: 'most models strongly overfit on a small set of verifiable constraints from the benchmarks that test these abilities... and are not able to generalize well to unseen output constraints' (Abstract).

Reward hacking / over-optimization on the constraint at the expense of the underlying task: 'Many models would prioritize the task, while IF-RLVR trained models tend to prioritize the constraint' (Sec 1); Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct specifically 'tends to prioritize the constraint over the instruction' (Sec 1) and its IF accuracy is 'the most negatively correlated with LLM-as-judge scores' among analyzed models (Sec 5).
Range-generalization failure: training on a variable range disjoint from the test range 'consistently scores lower' than same-range or wider-range training (Sec 4.3).

Difficulty clustering across models: 'most LLMs get the same easy instances right and the same hard instances wrong, which makes the creation of preference pairs more difficult' (Sec 4.6) — i.e., failure is not idiosyncratic per model but correlated across models on the same hard instances.

Base-model RLVR requires a special reasoning chat template to be evaluable at all; without post-training or the special template, 'base models before RLVR training cannot be evaluated as they don't have a chat template' (Table 6 caption).

Degradation on other capabilities from targeted IF-RLVR: 'targeted RLVR training for IF slightly harms other downstream evaluations, such as AlpacaEval 2, while staying comparable on others, such as GSM8K, MMLU and BBH' (Sec 4.5); IF-RLVR from a non-post-trained Qwen2.5 base shows very low AlpacaEval (1.1), GSM8K (15.3), and BBH (26.0) in Table 3, attributed to the base policy never having been post-trained.
one_shot_vs_multistep: IFBench's core evaluation is single forward-pass per turn in both its settings: single-turn (one concatenated task+constraint prompt, one completion) and multi-turn (a fixed 3-turn dialogue where the model produces exactly one final rewrite turn given prior context, with no scored intermediate correction loop) — Sec 2. The paper does not test an iterative/interactive refinement protocol where the model receives per-turn pass/fail feedback and retries; 'multi-turn' in this paper means constraint-isolation-across-context, not multi-attempt correction. Separately, IF-RLVR training (Sec 3, Sec 4.8) is explicitly a many-sample training-time procedure: GRPO samples 16 completions per prompt during training and updates the policy from verifiable rewards, and Table 7 shows models trained on a mix of single-turn and multi-turn data generalize better to both evaluation regimes than models trained on only one regime — i.e., what a model can do 'in one shot' at evaluation time is heavily conditioned on what regimes it saw during a many-sample RL training loop, but the evaluation-time protocol itself remains single-pass generation, never an interactive multi-turn correction loop with feedback.

relation_to_candidate.position: IFBench sits on the single-shot side of the family axis at evaluation time (single generation per turn, no interactive correction loop), matching c11. However, IFBench observes only the final generated text and applies a per-constraint programmatic verifier (a boolean/threshold check per constraint, e.g. counting occurrences, checking format markers) rather than requiring exact match against one specific canonical string; a given IFBench instance can have many valid surface strings that all pass. IFBench's 'multi-turn' setting technically extends the observed context across 3 turns of dialogue (task, a prior response, and a rewrite request) but is still scored as a single final generation, not an iterative correction loop with feedback between attempts. Output shape in IFBench is free-form natural-language text (a full response satisfying constraints) rather than c11's single fixed canonical string; verifier type is per-constraint boolean/programmatic pass-fail (sometimes with strict vs loose leniency, Sec 2) rather than c11's whole-string exact-match against a single reference canonicalizer output.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c11: Both tasks share the core paradigm of programmatically verifiable constraint-following: instructions encode deterministic, checkable rules (IFBench: e.g. 'Use at least N coordinating conjunctions', 'Nest parentheses at least 5 levels deep', CSV formatting with specific delimiters/quoting from Table 8's 'custom' group) that a Python function can check exactly, mirroring c11's reference-function-as-ceiling design. Both use synthetic/curated instance generation to guarantee contamination-proof, infinite pools (IFBench sources unseen WildChat prompts + newly authored constraints; c11 generates messy inputs programmatically). Both explicitly note that per-rule/per-constraint stratification lets pool accuracy climb incrementally and lets failures be attributed to specific violated rules (IFBench's category breakdown in Table 2 by case/detectable-format/keywords/length/count/format/words/sentence directly parallels c11's per-rule stratum diff attribution). IFBench's Appendix A 'custom' constraint group even includes CSV-generation-with-quoting-and-delimiter-rules instances (csv_quotes, csv_special_character) that are structurally close to c11's canonical-JSON/CSV-reserialization family, and 'date_format_list' (YYYY-MM-DD date reformatting) directly parallels c11's invented date-field canonicalization instances.
relation_to_candidate.differences: The decisive difference is the output/scoring contract: IFBench verifies whether a free-form response satisfies one or more independently-checkable constraints (partial credit is possible in principle per-constraint, though the paper reports whole-prompt correctness in Table 4's preference-data analysis), whereas c11 requires exact whole-string match against ONE canonical serialization with zero partial credit — a single wrong rule among 5-6 fails the entire instance. IFBench constraints largely govern surface text properties of an otherwise open-ended creative/informational response (count words, alternate syllables, use conjunctions) rather than c11's deterministic reduction of a given structured input to its unique canonical form; there is generally no single 'correct string' in IFBench, only a checkable property, while c11's ceiling actually IS a specific reference string per instance. IFBench's constraints are deliberately visible/instructable in natural language per-instance (the model is told the rule directly in-prompt each time, e.g. 'Use at least {N} different coordinating conjunctions'), whereas c11's house rules are the SAME fixed small rule set applied across many held-out instances, likely without being restated in full in every prompt if learned via demonstrations/GEPA optimization — closer to a latent, stable convention to be inferred/retained rather than an explicit per-instance instruction. IFBench also has a training/RLVR component entirely absent from c11's design (c11 is purely a prompting/inference-time task, not proposing RL training recipes).
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add: (1) a strictly single-string, whole-match ceiling with zero partial credit, which is a stronger and more brittle signal than IFBench's per-constraint boolean checks — useful for detecting subtle formatting slips (whitespace, quoting, key order) that IFBench's coarser constraint checks would not catch; (2) invented/house conventions that deliberately conflict with a real public standard (RFC 8785 JCS) rather than IFBench's constraints, which are novel but not framed as adversarial deviations from an existing canonical standard — this makes c11 a cleaner test of whether models can learn/apply an unfamiliar convention instead of falling back on memorized standard behavior, a contamination-resistance property IFBench achieves differently (via unseen constraint types) rather than via standard-deviation; (3) a structured-record/JSON canonicalization angle (key ordering, number formatting, escaping) that is largely absent from IFBench's prose-constraint focus, aside from the small CSV/date items in its 'custom' group; (4) diff-level attribution to a specific rule via string comparison against the one reference output, versus IFBench's per-category pass/fail aggregation.

corpus_claim: IFBench (Pyatkin et al., 2025) provides 58 new verifiable out-of-domain constraints with programmatic exact checks; it directly extends the constraint-following surge that c11's tie-breaker names.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper verbatim states IFBench 'consists of 58 new verifiable constraints that go beyond the 25 constraints included in IFEval' (Sec 2, 'IFBench' subsection), each paired with a Python verification function ('filtered constraints for the benchmark to those that can be easily paired with a verification function written in Python, making for reproducible evaluation and training tools', Sec 2), confirming both the '58 new' count and 'programmatic exact checks' framing. The 'out-of-domain' framing is also directly supported: constraints are explicitly held out/disjoint from training constraints ('We define a taxonomy of constraint templates, which we split into training and test constraints to prevent contamination', Sec 2) and the benchmark is explicitly framed as testing generalization to unseen constraints (Abstract, Sec 1). The claim that it 'extends the constraint-following surge' is a reasonable characterization given the paper's own Related Work section (Sec 6) situates itself among IFEval, FollowBench, InFoBench, and VFF as part of an active verifiable-instruction-following research line — this is a framing/positioning claim rather than a hard numeric fact, but nothing in the paper contradicts it.
baseline_relevant: Benchmark size and structure: IFBench is 300 prompts total, each instruction containing either 1 or 2 constraints (Sec 2); constraints span 7 categories (count, ratio, words, sentence, format, custom, copy), fully enumerated with example instruction text in Appendix A / Table 8 (58 rows listed, matching the paper's stated '58 new verifiable constraints') — useful as a concrete difficulty-dial reference (e.g. nesting depth 'at least 5 levels deep', ratio tolerances '±2%', counts parameterized by {N}).

Metrics: both 'strict' accuracy (exact constraint verification) and 'loose' accuracy are reported; loose accuracy 'additionally cleans the model's output by removing first/last lines and certain font modifiers' before verifying (Sec 2) — this is the paper's own precedent for freezing a trivial normalization step before scoring, directly relevant to c11's plan to freeze one trivial normalization (strip whitespace/code fences) in its Eval Config.

Observed floor/ceiling: frontier out-of-the-box models score below 50% on IFBench despite 80+% on IFEval (Sec 1) — establishes IFBench as a genuinely below-ceiling, non-saturated benchmark at the time of writing; after targeted IF-RLVR training, IFBench scores reach only 44.6-54.7 (Tables 3, 6), i.e., even dedicated training does not push scores much past ~55%, evidencing real headroom/difficulty in constraint-diversity generalization tasks of this shape.

Prompt-length/context stats (Appendix D.1): 'single-turn IFBench prompts have on average a length of 76 tokens and the multi-turn conversations provide an average input length of 408 tokens'; frontier models (pre-RLVR) generate responses averaging 2214 tokens on IFBench, while IF-RLVR-trained models generate much shorter responses averaging 210 tokens — a concrete data point on how RLVR training compresses output length, relevant if c11 wants to bound expected output length/verbosity.

Contamination control approach: constraints and test prompts are both held out/unreleased at data-construction time and human-annotated for prompt-constraint compatibility ('Every instance went through a human annotation process to verify the prompt and constraint compatibility', Sec 2); training constraints (IFTrain, Appendix B) are explicitly kept disjoint from IFBench's test constraints ('29 "unseen" constraint templates from IFTrain, which do not overlap with any of the test constraints', Sec 4.2) — directly analogous to c11's requirement that demo/minibatch coverage stay below full rule-table coverage to avoid copy-from-context leakage.

Data/code release status: code for IFBench is released at https://github.com/allenai/IFBench (Sec 1, footnote 2); the paper states it releases '29 additional new hand-annotated training constraints and verification functions, RLVR training prompts, and code' (Abstract) — i.e., this is an actively maintained, open corpus, not a closed/proprietary one, useful if c11's design wants to check for potential future contamination risk from public constraint lists.

Difficulty variability by constraint category: Table 2 breaks down IF-RLVR performance by category, showing large spread even after training — e.g. IF-RLVR (qwen2.5) row: case 93.3, detect-format 94.4, keywords 93.2, length 90.9, count 67.7, format 68.1, words 67.0, sentence 71.1 — the 'words' and 'sentence' IFBench categories remain hardest even post-training (Sec 4.5: 'Most of the IFEval categories seem saturated with IF-RLVR training (performance >90), while the IFBench categories leave room for improvement, such as the words and sentence categories') — useful signal for which rule types (lexical/positional constraints analogous to c11's ordinal/stopword tables) are intrinsically harder for models.
quotes: "We find that most models strongly overfit on a small set of verifiable constraints from the benchmarks that test these abilities, a skill called precise instruction following, and are not able to generalize well to unseen output constraints." (Abstract)
"we introduce IFBench with new, diverse, and challenging verifiable instruction following constraints where leading models such as GPT-4.1 or Claude 3.7 Sonnet score below 50%." (Sec 1, Introduction)

"consists of 58 new verifiable constraints that go beyond the 25 constraints included in IFEval [zhou2023instruction]... The final benchmark consists of 300 prompts... Each instruction in the benchmark contains either one or two constraints." (Sec 2, 'IFBench')

"Single-turn: The 'user' prompt consist of a general prompt with task t, concatenated with one or more output constraints c... Multi-turn: c is isolated from t in three turns... The model has to respond to the third turn, given all previous turns as context." (Sec 2, bullet list)

"With our new training techniques we improve the IFeval scores of a Tulu-3-8B model from 82.4 to 92.2, and the IFBench scores from 28.9 to 45.9. Similarly, IF-RLVR increases a Qwen-2.5-7B base model to scores of 87.8 (IFEval) and 54.7 (IFBench)..." (Sec 1, contribution 3)

"Removing the constraints from the length and the keywords categories harms IFEval performance the most, while removing constraints from the change cases and detectable format categories barely affect performance with the model achieving an IFEval accuracy of 89.65" (Sec 4.4)

"Completions from the base policy are scored higher by the LLM-as-judge than completions from the IF-RLVR trained model... with the base policy completions receiving an average score of 7 out of 10 and the IF-RLVR trained completions an average score of 6.4... The verifiable accuracy, though, is on average higher for the model that went through RLVR training." (Sec 5, 'Trade-offs between response quality and instruction following')

"the single-turn IFBench prompts have on average a length of 76 tokens and the multi-turn conversations provide an average input length of 408 tokens... the IF-RLVR trained models tend to generate shorter reponses, with an average token length of 210." (Appendix D.1)

corrections: Fixed a false cross-table 'match' claim in the Llama-3.1-Tulu-3-8B family key_results: the dossier had asserted the abstract's Tulu IFBench figure of 45.9 'matches the Table 6 instruct: tulu3-dpo after-IF-RLVR IFBench column' — this is incorrect. Table 6's instruct:tulu3-dpo after-IF-RLVR IFBench value is 44.6 (agreeing with Table 3, not with the abstract's 45.9). The value 45.9 in Table 6 actually belongs to the separate instruct:qwen2.5 column. Corrected the note to state that both Table 3 and Table 6 agree at 44.6 for Tulu, and the abstract's 45.9 is an unexplained ~1.3-point discrepancy rather than a second corroborating run.

Corrected 'baseline_relevant' claim that Appendix A / Table 8 lists '54 rows' of out-of-distribution test constraints — a direct row count of the source table gives 58 data rows, matching the paper's own stated count of 58 new verifiable constraints. Updated to '58 rows'.

Corrected the Table 2 category list in 'relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c11', which erroneously listed 'format' twice while dropping 'detectable-format' (a transcription slip from the table header case/detect./keywo./length/count/format/words/sent.). Fixed to list case/detectable-format/keywords/length/count/format/words/sentence.
